title: Standartox stx_query() SSD Grouping Factors
purpose: Factors to filter/aggregate stx_query() output into a per-species SSD dataset for ssdtools SSD fitting (ssd_fit_dists / ssd_hc). The same comparability principles also apply to drc dose-response fitting.
aggregation_principle: One value per species per chemical, under a comparable scenario; collapse replicate rows with the geometric mean.

tiers.label: must_match
tiers.description: Define the SSD universe; filter to a single level, never average across.

factors.rationale: One SSD describes the sensitivity of species to a single chemical. Use cas as the primary key (clean, no missing) and cross-check dtxsid.
factors.ssd_relevance: The dose axis of the fitted distribution must refer to one substance; mixing chemicals makes the fitted parameters and any HC5 meaningless.

factors.rationale: Effect-concentration endpoints (LC50/EC50 = XX50) must not be pooled with no/lowest-observed endpoints (NOEC/LOEC) or bioconcentration values; they measure different quantities.
factors.ssd_relevance: The SSD is a distribution of a single endpoint type across species; a homogeneous endpoint keeps the fitted CDF and derived hazard concentration interpretable.

factors.rationale: Different biological responses (mortality vs growth vs reproduction) reflect different sensitivities and should not be averaged together.
factors.ssd_relevance: A consistent response basis ensures each species value represents the same phenomenon, so the fitted distribution reflects real inter-species variation, not response heterogeneity.

factors.rationale: Active ingredient, formulation, total, and dissolved are different dose metrics and must not be mixed within one SSD.
factors.ssd_relevance: A single dose basis keeps the concentration axis on one scale, so fitted percentiles refer to a well-defined exposure metric.

valid_defaults.total: Dataset-dominant basis for the copper sulphate example (4353 rows); use as the default single basis when active ingredient is sparse.
valid_defaults.active ingredient: General-preference basis for cross-study consistency, but only 5 rows in this dataset.

factors.default_choice: Encoded as a Shiny input (allowed_conc_type); do not hard-code. Offer both 'total' and 'active ingredient' and let the widget pick a single basis per run.

factors.rationale: Values must share a common mass/volume basis (e.g. g/l) before any arithmetic; exclude area- or organism-based units (g/m2, g/organism).
factors.ssd_relevance: Homogeneous units keep the dose axis meaningful; mixed units produce a nonsensical fitted distribution and invalid HC5.

factors.rationale: Acute and chronic values must not be pooled. Bin to standard windows (e.g. 48/96 h acute) or split acute vs chronic into separate SSDs.
factors.ssd_relevance: Duration strongly shifts toxicity; a homogeneous duration class prevents the fitted distribution from being widened by a time confound.

factors.rationale: The SSD x-axis is species; aggregate to one value per tax_taxon. Guard against NA or genus-only rows.
factors.ssd_relevance: One value per species avoids pseudo-replication that would distort the fitted distribution and bias the HC5 toward heavily studied species.

factors.rationale: SSDs are usually built within a defined assemblage (e.g. aquatic animals, or fish-only). Handle combined labels such as 'algae || plant'. The SSD prompt further requires a derived taxon_group; see the top-level 'taxon_group_mapping' block for the explicit rules.
factors.ssd_relevance: A coherent assemblage keeps the distribution representative of the intended community and its protective threshold.

factors.rationale: Aquatic water exposures (static/renewal/flow-through) must not be mixed with oral/dermal routes (gavage, spray, dietary).
factors.ssd_relevance: Consistent exposure route ensures comparable delivered dose, so inter-species scatter reflects sensitivity rather than exposure differences.

tiers.label: optional
tiers.description: Refinements to use if data density allows; otherwise document.

factors.rationale: Biologically relevant but roughly 47% missing, so often too sparse to group on; may force pooling or a sensitivity check.
factors.ssd_relevance: Restricting to a life stage reduces within-species variability but can shrink the dataset below a fittable number of species.

factors.rationale: Freshwater vs marine SSDs are usually separated; not a clean column here and must be derived from tax_group / exposure context. Deferred for the current course task (not applied).
factors.ssd_relevance: Media consistency avoids mixing populations that differ systematically in tolerance, keeping the fit representative.

factors.rationale: Not a grouping key, but useful to de-duplicate the same result reported twice and to audit or weight studies.
factors.ssd_relevance: De-duplication before aggregation prevents a single study from being counted multiple times and skewing a species' geometric mean.
factors.prompt_step: data-quality duplicate check + curation step 7 (slice closest-to-target per species/reference)

taxon_group_mapping.description: Explicit rules for SSD-prompt curation step 5. Translate directly into a dplyr::case_when(). Standartox exports NO tax_class/tax_order/tax_phylum columns, so the mapping keys on tax_group first, then tax_family for invertebrates. Evaluate rules top to bottom; the first match wins; anything unmatched falls through to 'other'.

tax_group_rules.note: Bare 'plant' (10 rows) may not be a rooted aquatic macrophyte; mapped to 'other' per project decision.

invertebrate_family_rules.description: Applied only when tax_group == 'invertebrate'. Match on tax_family; unmatched invertebrate families (annelids, nematodes, echinoderms, cnidarians, ciliates, etc.) fall through to 'other'.
invertebrate_family_rules.note: The family lists are representative (derived from the copper sulphate example), not exhaustive. When a new chemical's data introduces unseen families, extend the lists by higher taxon: Crustacea -> crustacean, Mollusca -> mollusk, Rotifera -> rotifer, Insecta -> insect.

ssd_prompt_alignment.description: Maps ssd_analysis_prompt.md curation steps to the grouping factor / block they rely on.
ssd_prompt_alignment.step_4_concentration_type: tier1.concentration_basis (allowed_conc_type widget; total or active ingredient)
ssd_prompt_alignment.step_6_duration_windows: tier2.exposure_duration_and_class (taxon-specific acute windows in the prompt)
ssd_prompt_alignment.aggregation: aggregation_statistic + aggregation_group_key (geomean default; min/median offered via Shiny)

data_quality_gates: Drop -9999 sentinel values in concentration and duration before any calculation.
Drop physically impossible concentration extremes (unit-conversion artifacts) before aggregation.
Enforce a single concentration_unit per group (convert or filter to a common basis).
Decide handling of NA tax_taxon and NA concentration_type before grouping.
open_decisions: Acute vs chronic: build separate SSDs or one? This drives whether endpoint and duration are filters or facets. (SSD prompt builds a single acute SSD.)
Assemblage scope: aquatic animals only, fish-only, or whole community? Sets the tax_group filter.

Concentration basis: choose a single concentration_type per run via the allowed_conc_type widget; 'total' is the dataset-dominant basis here, 'active ingredient' is the general-preference basis where available.
Life stage: ignore as a grouping factor given ~47% missingness, or restrict to rows where present?
Aggregation statistic: geometric mean per species (default) vs. minimum (most sensitive) or median (SSD prompt exposes all three).
